questions.specification:
What is a likely cause of failure in this damaged structure?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/487f5b4f-c18e-4f9a-9df2-539f5331334a.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Fatigue refers to failure caused by repeated or cyclic loading over time. While fatigue may contribute to crack propagation, the presence of a geometric feature like a hole primarily points to stress concentration as the initiator.

Mechanical resonance occurs when a structure is subject to oscillating forces at its natural frequency. While it can lead to catastrophic failure, it is not the primary cause of localized cracking at a hole under static or simple tensile load.

Material incompatibility usually relates to chemical issues (e.g., galvanic corrosion) or thermal expansion mismatches between different materials, rather than structural failure due to geometric features.

Stress concentration (sometimes referred to as load concentration) occurs at locations where the cross-sectional area of a component changes abruptly, such as at a hole or notch. This causes a localized increase in stress that often results in crack initiation and structural failure.

questions.specification: Safety factors are most commonly applied in:

options.markscheme: Safety factors are critical in engineering and structural design to ensure that components can handle loads significantly higher than those expected in normal operation, ensuring user safety and preventing failure.

Packaging design focuses on containment, communication, and logistics; while it considers durability, the formal use of engineering safety factors is less central than in structural/mechanical design.

Electrical design uses derating, tolerances, and safety margins, but the term and method of 'safety factor' as a load/strength ratio is most commonly associated with structural and mechanical applications.
While safety is considered in textile manufacturing (e.g., flammability), the term 'safety factor' is specifically a mathematical ratio used primarily in structural and mechanical engineering.

parts.content: Explain how overload, material choice, size and shape contribute to structural failure. Give at least one explained point for each factor.
parts.markscheme:
- Overload occurs when applied forces exceed the ultimate strength of the material, causing sudden failure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Poor material choice may lead to inadequate strength, stiffness, or durability for the intended application. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Size affects the distribution of stress; for a given load, smaller cross-sections experience higher stress and may fail. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shape determines how forces are distributed or concentrated throughout the structure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sharp corners or weak joints act as stress concentrators where failure is likely to initiate. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Inadequate thickness or a lack of reinforcement can lead to structural collapse under load. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Award [1] for each valid explained point about how the factors contribute to failure, up to [6 max]. <marks>0 mark</marks>

questions.specification: Which of the following materials has the lowest Young's modulus?

options.markscheme: Rubber is an elastomer with a very low Young's modulus (typically $0.01$ to $0.1\text{ GPa}$), whereas the other materials are stiff solids with much higher moduli (Aluminium $\approx 70\text{ GPa}$, Glass $\approx 50\text{--}90\text{ GPa}$, Carbon fibre $\approx 230\text{--}600\text{ GPa}$).

questions.specification:
A structural engineer is evaluating four different steel beam profiles for use in a high-rise floor system. The primary design objective is to achieve the highest possible structural efficiency—defined as maximizing resistance to bending (stiffness) while minimizing the cross-sectional area (weight). 

Based on the technical data provided in the table below, which profile represents the most efficient selection for this application?

| Beam Profile | Cross-sectional area ($$A$$) [$$\text{mm}^2$$] | Second moment of area ($$I$$) [$$\text{mm}^4$$] |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Profile 1 | 1450 | 130,500 |
| Profile 2 | 1100 | 143,000 |
| Profile 3 | 950 | 171,000 |
| Profile 4 | 1200 | 132,000 |

options.markscheme: This profile has a stiffness-to-weight ratio ($$I/A$$) of 90. This is the lowest efficiency among the options, meaning it provides the least resistance to bending relative to its mass.
This profile has a stiffness-to-weight ratio ($$I/A$$) of 130. While more efficient than Profiles 1 and 4, it is less efficient than Profile 3.

Structural efficiency for a beam in bending is determined by the ratio of the second moment of area ($$I$$) to the cross-sectional area ($$A$$). Profile 3 has the highest ratio ($$171,000 / 950 = 180$$), making it the most efficient choice for maximizing stiffness while minimizing weight.
This profile has a stiffness-to-weight ratio ($$I/A$$) of 110. It requires more material than Profile 3 while providing significantly less resistance to bending.

questions.specification: Failure due to material fatigue in a structure can be mitigated by:

options.content: Increasing the surface roughness of components
options.markscheme: Increasing surface roughness creates more stress concentration points at the surface, which accelerates crack initiation and decreases fatigue life.

options.content: Using materials with lower endurance limits
options.markscheme: Materials with lower endurance limits will fail at lower stress levels when subjected to cyclic loading, which does not mitigate fatigue.

options.content: Designing components with sharp internal corners
options.markscheme: Sharp internal corners act as stress raisers, significantly increasing local stress and promoting the development of fatigue cracks.

options.content: Optimising material choice and safety factors
options.markscheme: Selecting materials with appropriate fatigue strength and endurance limits, combined with applying safety factors to account for cyclic loading uncertainties, helps prevent fatigue failure.

questions.specification:
1. A team of design engineers is developing an outdoor viewing platform for a national park. Their design includes a cantilever beam for the overhanging section, uses mild steel components for structural support, and must meet strict safety regulations due to heavy visitor use. The team is evaluating structural behaviour through diagrams, materials testing, and past case studies of failures.

The engineers are using the following visuals in their analysis: 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9903eb0f-9064-41c2-89db-3b3974519b7e)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/cad497a4-2d05-46e2-8210-4ee57178118e)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/dd26bfef-f1ca-42cc-ba97-39094fe7ea1c)

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 1**. Describe the direction and location of the key forces acting on the cantilever beam.
parts.markscheme:
- The point load at the free end acts vertically downward. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The fixed end provides a vertical reaction force and a counterclockwise moment. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 1**. Explain two ways to increase the structural rigidity of the cantilever beam.
parts.markscheme:
- Use a material with a higher Young’s Modulus to reduce deflection. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Increase the cross-sectional height or add trusses to improve moment of inertia. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 2**. Identify the region of the graph that represents plastic deformation.
parts.markscheme: - The plastic region begins just after the yield point and continues until the ultimate tensile strength. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to Figure 2. Calculate the Young’s Modulus if a tensile stress of 2000 MPa causes a strain of 0.01.
parts.markscheme: - $E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}=\frac{2000\ \text{MPa}}{0.01}=200{,}000\ \text{MPa}$ or $200\ \text{GPa}$. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 3**. Suggest one likely cause of the bridge’s structural failure.
parts.markscheme:
- Material fatigue, corrosion, or poor maintenance may have weakened critical joints. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The bridge may have experienced loads beyond its design capacity. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 3**. Explain how applying a safety factor (SF) during design could have prevented this failure.
parts.markscheme:
- A higher SF ensures the structure can withstand unexpected overloads or deterioration. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- SF increases reliability by keeping operational stress well below failure limits. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What is the purpose of diagrammatically representing forces within structures?

options.content: To visualise load paths and improve structural design
To estimate material quantities and overall project cost from component dimensions
To show the external appearance and aesthetic style of the structure
To communicate where forces act and how they are balanced so internal forces can be determined

questions.specification:
What structural concept is shown in this bridge truss diagram?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/4dae6c2f-845d-4e9d-9efb-ff55677baa74.jpeg)

options.markscheme: Incorrect. While thermal expansion is a design consideration for bridges, the diagram specifically illustrates the distribution of internal mechanical forces (tension and compression) within the truss members.

options.content: Distribution of tensile and compressive forces
options.markscheme: Correct. The diagram shows a truss bridge where the applied load is distributed through the members, resulting in some being in a state of tension (pulling apart) and others in compression (pushing together).

Incorrect. Young's modulus is a material property determined by experimental testing of material samples. This diagram shows a macroscopic structural analysis rather than a laboratory measurement technique.

options.content: Shear force distribution in a beam
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Shear force distribution is typically represented using shear force diagrams for beams. This diagram focuses on axial forces in truss members (tension and compression), not beam shear.

questions.specification: When designing a ladder, which structure-related factor is most critical?

options.markscheme: Surface finish/paint thickness mainly affects corrosion resistance and appearance; it has little effect on the ladder’s load-bearing capacity compared with geometry and material stiffness.

options.content: Rung tread pattern and grip texture
options.markscheme: Tread pattern/texture primarily affects user grip and slip resistance, not the primary structural integrity of the ladder under load.

Geometry (e.g., rail cross-section, rung spacing, and overall angle) and material stiffness (resistance to elastic deformation) are primary determinants of structural integrity and stability under load.
This is an economic and manufacturing constraint rather than a structural property determining load capacity and stiffness.

questions.specification: Why do many structures fail during use?

options.content: Incorrect material choice, geometry, or overloading
options.markscheme: Structural failure is primarily caused by technical factors including selecting materials unsuitable for the load, design geometries that concentrate stress, or using the structure beyond its design capacity (overloading). <marks>A1</marks>
Packaging design focuses on the protection and containment of a product during transport and storage, rather than its structural integrity during its functional use.

While environmental durability is a factor, it is often considered a subset of material choice (selecting a material that cannot withstand its environment). Option B remains the most comprehensive answer.

Manufacturing defects and poor quality control can introduce weaknesses (e.g., voids, poor joints, dimensional inaccuracies) that reduce load capacity, but this is less comprehensive than material choice, geometry, or overloading as a general explanation.

questions.specification: How does Young’s modulus differ from tensile strength?

options.content: It is a measure of stiffness, whereas tensile strength relates to the point of failure.
options.markscheme: Young’s modulus ($E$) is a measure of the stiffness of an elastic material and is defined as the ratio of stress to strain. Tensile strength is the maximum stress that a material can withstand while being stretched or pulled before necking or failure occurs.

options.content: It predicts the long-term fatigue of a material.
options.markscheme: Fatigue is the weakening of a material caused by cyclic loading, which is a different mechanical property from the elastic stiffness measured by Young's modulus.

options.content: It estimates the shape memory effect of a material.
options.markscheme: Shape memory is a property of certain materials (like Nitinol) to return to a pre-deformed shape when heated, which is unrelated to the definition of Young's modulus.

options.content: It relates to the thermal resistance of a material.
options.markscheme: Thermal resistance is a measure of a temperature difference by which an object or material resists a heat flow, which is a thermal property rather than a mechanical one.

parts.content: Explain two benefits and two limitations of using force diagrams when designing structures.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the benefit of visualising load distribution <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the benefit of predicting structural response <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the limitation of assuming idealised conditions <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the limitation of not accounting for material imperfections <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each well-explained benefit and limitation.</note>

parts.content: Identify two types of structural failure and briefly describe the cause of each.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify buckling as a structural failure caused by compressive forces on slender components <marks>A1</marks>
- Identify fracture as a structural failure caused by exceeding the tensile strength of the material <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award [1] for each correct type and cause.</note>

questions.specification: Which property is indicated by a high Young modulus?
What does the Young modulus primarily measure in a material?

parts.content: Describe how force diagrams are constructed and how they assist in evaluating beam performance.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the type of beam and the nature of its supports. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Apply** external loads and calculate the reaction forces at the supports using equilibrium equations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Calculate** the internal shear forces and bending moments at key points along the beam. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Draw** the shear force and bending moment diagrams based on the calculated values. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** the maximum values of shear and moment and identify their specific locations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** how these diagrams assist in identifying potential failure points and optimizing material reinforcement. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each step or benefit clearly explained up to [6 max].</note>

questions.specification: What does it mean if a structure is under-engineered?

options.content: It is oversized for its purpose
options.markscheme: This describes an over-engineered structure, which is more robust than necessary.

options.content: It fails easily due to insufficient design
options.markscheme: Under-engineering occurs when a structure is designed or built below the required standard, making it prone to failure.
Environmental testing is a standard part of the design process and does not define under-engineering.
The use of premium materials is often associated with high-quality or over-engineered products, not under-engineered ones.

questions.specification:
Which of the following factors would most influence the factor of safety for the gear set shown?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXdXxYwMc3VrlWEII0DnwGaLijsTzN7k2O0XsHrM3hlQJZ0SvZeTq1wFyOdwbnjbzmF6t2la8Wb_s4Q518AMwHp85KFjy5LArCKlwFBegBahxKMCickxSd8l_XI4x7uTzEEZiUV5bw?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.content: Material and heat treatment of the gear teeth
Applied torque / transmitted load in operation

questions.specification: Designers must consider how structural systems behave under load and how to ensure safety and durability. The following figures illustrate different real-world structures.

parts.content:
**Figure 1:** A stone arch bridge over a river


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/024c2382-9d97-4d7f-8c26-d7d1d6bc3bcd)

Refer to **Figure 1**. Identify the type of force primarily resisted by the stone arch bridge.
parts.markscheme: - Compression. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 1**. State one advantage of using an arch structure in bridge design.
parts.markscheme:
- Distributes load efficiently through compression. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Requires minimal tensile resistance in the material. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2:** A collapsed bookshelf with books scattered on the floor


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d25f1040-0f3d-447b-9198-504beb8637cf)

Refer to **Figure 2**. Suggest two possible reasons for the failure of the bookshelf.
parts.markscheme:
- Overloading due to excessive weight of books. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Poor quality fasteners or joints. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Refer to **Figure 2**. Explain how a designer could modify the design to improve structural performance.
parts.markscheme:
- Increase thickness or material strength of shelves. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Add vertical supports in the centre to reduce span. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improve fixings to ensure better load distribution. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3:** A scaffolding structure assembled at a construction site


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b6c1c2a4-4af1-4027-9a85-240795bf5ee0)

Refer to **Figure 3**. Explain four reasons why scaffolding is constructed using modular components.
parts.markscheme:
- Modular parts allow for flexible design to fit different spaces. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Easy to assemble, disassemble, and transport. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Components can be reused across multiple projects. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows for safe and scalable height adjustments. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: With reference to all three figures, evaluate how understanding structural systems can help prevent failure in both permanent and temporary structures.
parts.markscheme:
- Figure 1 shows how arch bridges manage compressive forces to avoid collapse. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 2 illustrates the risk of ignoring load limits and poor design in furniture. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Figure 3 highlights how modular scaffolding must be properly braced and anchored. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Understanding load paths helps designers avoid tension/compression mismatch. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Proper material selection and joint design improve durability and safety. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Temporary structures also require engineering principles to avoid hazards. <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What does a force diagram of a beam typically include?

parts.content: Compare stress-strain graphs of ductile and brittle materials and discuss their implications in product design.
parts.markscheme:
- State that ductile materials exhibit a large plastic region before fracture, allowing for significant deformation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- State that brittle materials fracture or fail suddenly shortly after the elastic limit is passed, with negligible plastic deformation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Contrast the graph profiles, noting that ductile materials show a non-linear region after the yield point, whereas brittle materials typically have a linear stress-strain relationship until fracture and a much smaller strain at failure. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain the design implication that ductile materials provide a safety warning through visible deformation (necking) before ultimate failure occurs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Explain that ductile materials are preferred for applications involving dynamic loads or where energy absorption (toughness) is required. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Discuss the trade-off for brittle materials, which may be selected for their high stiffness but require careful design to avoid catastrophic failure under tensile or impact loads. <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Award [1] for each well-explained comparison or implication up to [6 max].</note>

parts.content: Describe how FEA (Finite Element Analysis) can be used to evaluate structural performance.
parts.markscheme:
- FEA involves breaking down complex structures into smaller elements for easier analysis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Each element is analysed individually for stress, strain, and deformation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Designers use the analysis to identify potential weak points before physical testing <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The results allow for design optimization before the manufacturing process begins <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award [1] for each relevant point of explanation.</note>

questions.specification: Which of the following pairs best represents similar structures found in both nature and engineered designs?

options.markscheme: Spider webs and suspension bridges are both examples of structures that primarily use tensile forces and flexible cables (or threads) to support loads over a span.
Both use cellular geometry, but this pair compares two engineered structural concepts rather than one from nature and one from engineering.
Both act mainly in compression, but the trunk is a biological structure with complex anisotropic behavior; the analogy is less direct than cable-tension structures spanning a gap.

Both are shell structures that carry loads largely through membrane stresses, but the match is less general/typical than the spider web–suspension bridge analogy in terms of primary spanning mechanism.

questions.specification:
Which of the following mechanical interactions best represents the force behaviour in this type of product design?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXeiLdx03GutalAatG-G5KJkTpredVETdueErbysqGNN6jXyk7cuzW28kRYICTcQv_8QR1TZGcJJi2c4KZUMPZPafRa3_x3yHM5WGmqIRLfHAiG4aL_nRkY5waohx8us8e5GSrMUMQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
Why are safety factors generally set above $1$?

options.content: To ensure the structure has a factor of exactly $1.0$ for maximum efficiency
To ensure function in unexpected load conditions
To allow for material degradation and variations in material properties over time

questions.specification: A school engineering team is designing a wooden bridge model for a physics competition. The bridge must span 1 meter and support a heavy load at its center without failing. The students are testing different materials and cross-sectional designs to improve strength and stiffness.

parts.content:
The students are looking at the following: **Figure 1**: A basic beam bending diagram


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/18024363-d3a9-4625-a549-e12adef46862)

Refer to **Figure 1**. Where does the maximum bending moment occur in the beam?
parts.markscheme:
At the midpoint of the span, directly under the applied central load. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for correct identification of peak moment location.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2**: A stress–strain graph comparing different materials.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ec5361c3-2606-4fb2-be33-c1f3b0798d02)

Refer to **Figure 2**. Identify two differences in material behavior between steel and wood.
parts.markscheme:
Steel shows a clear yield point, while wood does not. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Wood fractures at a lower stress level than steel. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per correct difference in stress–strain behavior.*

parts.content: Using the stress–strain graph in **Figure 2**, evaluate the suitability of steel and wood for the bridge design.
parts.markscheme:
Steel has a higher Young’s Modulus, making it stiffer. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Wood is more elastic but fails at lower stress levels. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Steel can support greater loads before yielding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Wood may be suitable for light loads or budget constraints. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Steel’s ductility gives warning before failure; wood fails more suddenly. <marks>1 mark</marks>

For maximum strength and safety, steel is more appropriate. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per justified point with clear use of Figure 2, up to [6 max].*

parts.content:
**Figure 3**: A free-body diagram of forces on a truss bridge


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/92cee37f-a199-4301-86ac-26af61f31ec0)

Refer to **Figure 3**. Describe three forces acting on the bridge structure.
parts.markscheme:
Compression in the top horizontal member. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Tension in the bottom horizontal member. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Vertical reaction forces at the two supports. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each correct force identified and described, up to [3 max].*

parts.content: Explain how changing the cross-sectional shape of bridge beams improves structural performance.
parts.markscheme:
I-beams and box sections increase the moment of inertia. <marks>1 mark</marks>

A higher moment of inertia reduces bending for the same load. <marks>1 mark</marks>

Material is distributed further from the neutral axis, increasing strength. <marks>1 mark</marks>

This allows stronger bridges without using more material. <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each valid explanation up to [4 max].*

questions.specification:
What do these diagram arrows represent in structural analysis?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXfyaRmGqZP1z0g0wcly1-X9irK3SG0huebvVWw9UvB9BANXcLQ8zpzgJG2FG5YOk7EFAoZMUg4YgnrG-Vt-xndmZsNyzLhYJz2lymHGIQUgvu6_W-TR_U11tC228R_9f73_TKZS?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

What structural property likely failed in this snapped material?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXes_fSiiHC-9dbtoqcK0q3iVbxQxq-Dt7VfhACGhKL_IMkk4lQhhPRQdKvxidMvoImLRo1JsugQglrji5OKdo8Ya_zTDux4VixIKkC0-1xoGnET9w64fLDSWQrtLS1cPUgjPpNlLQ?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)

options.markscheme: The ultimate tensile strength (UTS) is the maximum stress that a material can withstand while being stretched or pulled before failing or breaking. If a material snaps under tension, its UTS has been exceeded.

questions.specification: A structure fails because it was too thin to support the applied force. What failure factor is this?
Which statement about safety factors (SFs) is most accurate?
Which best explains how Young's modulus affects product design?

options.content: It helps assess how much a material bends under load
options.markscheme: Young's modulus is a measure of the stiffness of an elastic material. In product design, it is used to calculate how much a component will deflect or bend when subjected to a specific load, which is essential for ensuring structural performance.
Thermal expansion is governed by the coefficient of thermal expansion, which is independent of Young's modulus.

options.content: It determines how easily a material plastically deforms before fracture (ductility)
options.markscheme: Ductility relates to plastic deformation before fracture and is assessed by measures such as percentage elongation or reduction in area, not by Young's modulus (elastic stiffness).

options.content: It indicates the energy a material can absorb before fracturing (toughness)
options.markscheme: Toughness is the ability to absorb energy up to fracture (area under the stress–strain curve), whereas Young's modulus describes elastic stiffness (slope of the elastic region).

questions.specification:
What does this diagram illustrate about structural beams?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/05536441-f186-46ac-8912-65be11a0f78b.jpeg)

## Question

The design of beams and supports often requires:

Which structural design consideration is shown in this bicycle frame?

![](https://lh7-rt.googleusercontent.com/docsz/AD_4nXcnuSghvxPrCu_HKOH5AaGVcO0rUpxz0SOyuZCNA9iaTR6Q17Qa9oSaIishb5dG24Es6LPk3_NFgb51VGmnhJoR9qqEAITfXsdEGe2rIfQuvQGaedcgfbSwtYPPfcpL85vUl3w?key=gz2IFjHMQBPKFRswYwU8xQ)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a1f49837-eb3a-41d9-a304-0023839b4fac)

options.markscheme: While the image visually maps loads using Finite Element Analysis (FEA), the design consideration itself is the awareness of where stress is concentrated.

The image shows a Finite Element Analysis (FEA) plot, which is used by designers to identify and manage areas of high stress (stress concentrations) to ensure structural integrity and optimize material use.
Modular joint flexibility refers to joints designed for movement or reconfiguration, which is not indicated by the stress analysis shown.
Pre-stressed beams involve internal stresses introduced during manufacture to counteract external loads; the image shows stress distribution under load, not pre-stressing.

questions.specification: A team of product engineers is analysing different household objects to understand how common structural forces affect real-world designs. They are documenting how materials behave under tension, compression, bending, and torsion.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a simple wooden dining table with four legs.



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/5d2517d3-099e-4f4f-aca4-9dfa64ccfbfe)

i.) State the main structural force acting on each of the table legs when the table is in use.
parts.markscheme:
Compression <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for correct identification of compression as the main force.*

parts.content: ii.) Explain how cross-bracing under the tabletop could improve the table’s structural stability.
parts.markscheme:
Cross-bracing prevents lateral movement <marks>1 mark</marks>

It distributes forces more evenly and resists shear <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for describing the purpose, [1] for explaining its structural effect.*

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a metal wrench twisting a bolt.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/d8863eb0-3086-4402-b470-efb3ea05dd9a)
 i.) Identify the type of stress the wrench experiences and describe how it acts on the material.
parts.markscheme:
Torsional stress <marks>1 mark</marks>


Acts by twisting the material around its axis <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for stress type, [1] for correct description of its action.*

parts.content: ii.) Suggest one reason why forged steel is used to manufacture wrenches.
parts.markscheme:
Forged steel has high torsional and tensile strength <marks>1 mark</marks>


It can withstand repeated twisting without failing <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for material property, [1] for relevant application.*

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a wooden plank supported at both ends with a weight placed in the centre.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/29dba57a-192b-488d-8066-b36fd5e73335)
i.) Explain the two types of stress occurring on the top and bottom surfaces of the plank.
parts.markscheme:
Top surface experiences compressive stress due to downward force <marks>1 mark</marks>


Bottom surface experiences tensile stress as it stretches under the load <marks>1 mark</marks>


These opposite stresses result in bending <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] for each correct identification and explanation up to [3 max].*

parts.content: ii.) Evaluate how a designer might adapt this type of structure to reduce bending deformation in future products.
parts.markscheme:
Increase thickness of the plank to reduce deflection <marks>1 mark</marks>


Use stiffer materials like laminated wood or metal <marks>1 mark</marks>


Add central support to shorten the span and reduce bending moment <marks>1 mark</marks>


Change cross-section shape (e.g. I-beam) to increase moment of inertia <marks>1 mark</marks>


Reinforce with tension cables or external supports <marks>1 mark</marks>


Use stress simulation to predict weak points and optimise design <marks>1 mark</marks>

*Award [1] per developed point up to [6 max].*

questions.specification: What type of diagram is most effective for showing internal forces in a structure?

options.markscheme: A bending moment diagram shows variation of bending moment along a member, not a general representation of all forces acting on a body.
A shear force diagram shows variation of shear force along a member, not a general representation of all forces acting on a body.
A free-body diagram isolates a body or member and shows the forces (including internal forces where relevant) acting on it, making it effective for analysing internal forces in structures.
A stress-strain graph shows a material’s mechanical behaviour under loading, not the force system within a structure.

questions.specification: A team of student designers is studying how everyday furniture and products are affected by basic structural forces like tension, compression, shear, torsion, and bending. They are using familiar objects to visualise how these forces work.

parts.content:
**Figure 1** shows a wooden bookshelf with books placed on it.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/c4390689-7521-4f01-a1c0-3f09f3dae184)

Identify the main type of structural force acting on the horizontal shelf.
parts.markscheme: - Bending <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how bending stress could cause the shelf to fail over time.
parts.markscheme:
- Continuous downward force causes the shelf to deflect in the middle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Over time, this can lead to permanent deformation or cracking <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 2** shows a metal suspension bridge with cables.



![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/835d8aa1-b97f-4389-8d74-e489bd23fe6f)

Define tensile force and identify where it occurs in the bridge structure.
parts.markscheme:
- Tensile force is a pulling force that stretches a material <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It occurs in the suspension cables of the bridge <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest a reason why steel is often used for cables in suspension bridges.
parts.markscheme:
- Steel has high tensile strength and resists stretching <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It also has durability and can withstand environmental stress <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
**Figure 3** shows a plastic chair with a person sitting on it.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/81e6421a-3621-46de-a5e2-3a06c355d4e7)

Explain **three** different types of structural forces acting on the legs of the chair.
parts.markscheme:
- Compression: from the downward force of the person’s weight <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shear: where the leg joints connect to the seat, resisting lateral sliding <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Bending: if the load is not evenly distributed, causing legs to flex slightly <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how knowledge of structural forces helps designers improve the safety and durability of home furniture.
parts.markscheme:
- Helps choose appropriate materials based on expected forces (e.g. steel for tension, plastic for flexibility) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Guides reinforcement design (e.g. cross-bracing to reduce shear or buckling) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Allows testing of stress limits to prevent product failure or injury <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improves product lifespan by reducing fatigue or permanent deformation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables safer use by accounting for worst-case user scenarios (e.g. uneven loading) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps meet industry safety standards through better structural performance <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: What scenario justifies increasing a safety factor?

What is the main design reason why this climbing harness has a high factor of safety?

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/5af67353-75e5-4b87-b1cc-879b6c2a3a8a.jpeg)

options.content: To reduce cost through mass production
To improve aesthetic appeal for marketing
To accommodate wear and repeated use over time

questions.specification: Which of the following best describes why structures are relevant to product design?

options.content: They contribute to both function and aesthetic integrity
They enable faster mass production of consumer goods
They influence the market demand for ergonomic furniture
They reduce a product’s environmental impact

topic.title: B3.2 Structural systems application and selection (HL only)
